sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient presented with a 3-month history of intermittent cough, which initially started while loading horses and resolved after a cold, but recurred and prompted a visit to their primary care physician, who diagnosed bronchitis and referred them to me for further evaluation of a lung nodule found on a chest X-ray.
The patient has a history of biking and exercising regularly, which has not resulted in typical illness.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently controlled with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, and has been a non-smoker for 7 years.
The patient's family history is notable for their mother having breast cancer, but no history of lung cancer.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently controlled with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, and a history of smoking, which they quit 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 124/76, heart rate is 70, respiratory rate is 16, and oxygen saturation on room air is 98%.
Physical exam: The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilaterally, and the heart has a regular rate and rhythm with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The patient has a little bit of swelling in the knuckles on both hands, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.

Test Results: The patient had a chest CT scan that showed a solitary 2-centimeter nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which appears smooth in appearance and has no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule that requires further evaluation.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; History of smoking; Family history of breast cancer.
Plan:
I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for the patient's lung function.
I will also schedule a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
I will recommend video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule, which will involve three small incisions on the side of the patient's right chest and general anesthesia.
The patient will be admitted to the hospital for one night and discharged the following day.
The patient should continue to follow up with their rheumatologist and continue their medication therapy as previously outlined.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in a few minutes to sign paperwork and begin the treatment process.
